topic.what: NEJM review of pernicious anemia as an autoimmune disease targeting gastric parietal cells and intrinsic factor, leading to vitamin B12 malabsorption and megaloblastic anemia
topic.how: Expert review of immunological mechanisms (anti-parietal-cell and anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies), clinical presentation, diagnostic workup (Schilling test, serological markers), and B12 replacement therapy
topic.why_matters: Pernicious anemia is the classic and most severe hematological consequence of autoimmune gastritis; understanding it as an autoimmune gastric disease (not merely a hematological condition) is essential for proper diagnosis and long-term management

characteristics.what_it_measures: Dual autoantibody targeting of parietal cells and intrinsic factor
characteristics.value: Anti-parietal-cell antibodies destroy oxyntic mucosa; anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies block B12 binding and absorption
characteristics.vs_baseline: Established the immunological basis distinguishing autoimmune from dietary or malabsorptive B12 deficiency
characteristics.context: Published in an era when Schilling test was the gold-standard diagnostic; serological approaches have since largely replaced it

good_for: Understanding the immunological basis of pernicious anemia
Diagnostic approach to megaloblastic anemia with suspected autoimmune etiology
Linking pernicious anemia to the broader autoimmune gastritis disease spectrum
not_for: Non-autoimmune B12 deficiency (dietary, surgical, ileal disease)
requires: Serological testing for anti-intrinsic-factor and anti-parietal-cell antibodies
B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine levels for diagnosis

novelty: Landmark NEJM synthesis reframing pernicious anemia as an organ-specific autoimmune disease rather than a purely hematological entity
Integrated the serological, histopathological, and clinical dimensions of autoimmune gastric destruction
open_problems: Schilling test (used at time of publication) is no longer widely available; replacement diagnostic algorithms needed
Genetic susceptibility loci for pernicious anemia remain incompletely mapped
Optimal screening strategy for gastric neoplasia in pernicious anemia patients was not addressed
